title: STATS19 reported road casualties: LA region of Cambridge
text:
This is a report for the Local Authority (LA) region of Cambridge, which can be reproduced for any LA by cloning the repository at https://github.com/blaisekelly/stats19_stats. #The LA boundary is shown on this plot of the cycle network (from OSM data processed with the osmactive package).
In this report all serious and slight casualty data is based on the adjusted data.
In 2024, Cambridge had 213 collisions involving all casualty types resulting in 243 casualties reported to the police. This is the 206th highest of 374 Local Authorities in Great Britain (216th per capita) and a decrease in collisions of 22 but a decrease in total casualties of 20. For fatal it ranked 348th ( 300th per capita) with 0 casualties down on 2023. For KSIs it ranked 256th (237th per capita) with 49 casualties down on 2023. For serious injuries it ranked 244th (223rd per 1000) with 49 casualties down on 2023. For slight injuries it ranked 187th (204th per 1000) with 194 casualties down on 2023.













Compared to 5 years ago collisions are up by 11, total casualties up by 18, fatalities down by 1, KSI casualties down by 1, serious injuries down by 0 and slight injuries up by 19.









For cyclists there were 124 collisions resulting in 124 cyclist casualties reported to the police. This is the 22nd highest of 374 Local Authorities in Great Britain (11th per capita) and a decrease in collisions of 8 but a decrease in total casualties. For fatal it ranked 216th (189th per capita) with 0 cyclist casualties down on 2023. For KSIs it ranked 26th ( 9th per capita) with 30 casualties down on 2023. For serious injuries it ranked 26th (9th per 1000) with 30 cyclist casualties down on 2023. For slight injuries it ranked 22nd (12th per 1000) with 94 cyclist casualties down on 2023.













Compared to 5 years ago collisions involving cyclists are up by 16, total casualties up by 13, fatalities down by 1, KSI casualties up by 7, serious injuries up by 8 and slight injuries up by 6.









For pedestrians there were 19 collisions resulting in 24 pedestrian casualties reported to the police. This is the 242nd highest of 374 Local Authorities in Great Britain (250th per capita) and a decrease in collisions of 11 and a decrease in total casualties. For fatal it ranked 286th (247th per capita) with 0 pedestrian casualties down on 2023. For KSIs it ranked 310th (291st per capita) with 5 casualties down on 2023. For serious injuries it ranked 299th (281st per capita) with 5 pedestrian casualties down on 2023. For slight injuries it ranked 206th (214th per capita) with 19 pedestrian casualties down on 2023.













Compared to 5 years ago collisions involving pedestrians are down by 10, total casualties down by 7, fatalities down by 0, KSI casualties down by 5, serious injuries down by 5 and slight injuries down by 2.











The interactive map at this link shows the location of all collisions between 2010 and 2024 sized by severity of the casualties and coloured by the speed limit of the road it took place on and dissagregated by casualty using the radio buttons. Clicking on each casualty provides further information about the casualty. (note: sometimes when first loading the casualty types need to be toggled a few times before they show the correct corresponding dots, clue is that bus occupants don’t make up many casualties) # {r, fig.width=100%} # #| echo: false # knitr::include_graphics("https://blaisekelly.github.io/stats19_stats/outputs/Bristol/maps/cas_location_Speed%20limit.html") #  #
Grouping by street, using Open Street Maps data, the map at this link shows the collisions for each street coloured by KSI and dissagregated by year. Each street can be clicked to activate a pop-up with more details.



Casualty data was matched to 594 road links from OpenStreetMap for the period 2010 to 2024, covering 1174 collisions. The road with the highest number of collisions was Mill Road (28), followed by Mill Road (16). Across 5 years the peak year for collisions on mapped roads was 2022 with 271 collisions. By casualty type the most common on the OSM network was Cyclist (643 collisions). The table below shows the highest 20 roads by collision count for the full period. Click a header to sort the column.






The plots below show plots of collisions of the 2 roads with the most collisions in Cambridge: Mill Road and Mill Road. The plot on the left represents the casualties and the plot on the right vehicles involved. To see a plot larger, right click and either save or “open in new tab”.























In Cambridge, according to OSM data the majority of roads are 20mph (56%). Between 2010 and 2024 the majority of collisions (820) happened on 30mph roads. (100)% of fatalities happened on 30mph roads, which make up 19, NA% of roads in the LA. 211 casualties were seriously injured on 30mph roads. Collision rates per km are highest for 50mph roads, fatality rates highest on NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NAmph roads, serious injury rates highest on NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NAmph and slight injury rates 50mph. The collision rate on 20mph roads was 0, NA% lower than the highest roads (50mph) and fatality rates 0, NA% of 30mph roads and NaN, NA% of the highest fatality rate (NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NAmph) roads. KSI rates were highest on NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NAmph roads, 8.8, NA times higher than 20mph roads.
The chart below shows percentage of fatalities by road speed limit.









Applying the 30mph fatality rate (0.261, NA) fatalities per 1000 collisions to the number of 20mph collisions would give 1, NA fatalities. Applying the per km rate of 12.75, NA to the 20mph road network would give a staggering 10, NA fatalities.

Source: DfT STATS19 and OSM













The top left plot below show the distribution of the population by LSOA zone, the most granular population data available for the whole of Great Britain. In the top right are the number of total collisions by LSOA zone, calculated by intersecting the crash data with the LSOA shape files and the bottom two plots shows the home LSOA of casualties and vehicles involved in collisions in the city.












The Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) is the official measure of relative deprivation in England and is part of a suite of outputs that form the Indices of Deprivation (IoD) given as a decile in the STATS19 casualty and vehicle data for the casualty and drivers home LSOA respectively. It follows an established methodological framework in broadly defining deprivation to encompass a wide range of an individual’s living conditions. It is important in road traffic collision analysis because it says something about where harm is distributed in society. In Cambridge, 72.4% of the population fall into the least deprived IMD decile. 68.4, NA% of casualties injured between 2010 and 2024 fall into the least, NA deprived IMD decile. NA% of casualty data had home LSOA.



The relationship between deprivation and road casualty risk is marked in Cambridge. The most deprived IMD decile (decile 1) accounts for 6% of casualties (69), while the least deprived (decile 10) accounts for 13% (149). Residents of the most deprived areas are 0.5 times more likely to be a casualty than those in the least deprived decile. The decile with the highest number of casualties overall is decile 9 with 17 casualties (1.5%). As noted above, NA% of casualty records had a home LSOA that could be matched to an IMD decile, where as only xx of driver data had data on home location.
The charts below show a breakdown of the ages and sex number of KSI and IMD Decil of casualties in Cambridge.












Analysis of KSI casualties by IMD decile in Cambridge reveals that decile 9 (least deprived) accounts for the highest KSI total across casualty types combined, with approximately 55 KSI casualties. Cyclists represent the highest KSI casualty type overall (~138). Pedestrian KSI peaks in decile 8, while cyclist KSI is highest in decile 9. By age, the most affected groups are 70+ for pedestrians, 30-39 for cyclists, and 16-19 for motorcyclists. Males account for approximately 155 KSI casualties compared to 106 for females.



Between LSOA and Local Authority are Medium Super Output Levels (MSOA). Because of their relatively consistent size across the country, they are a useful unit for analysing data about small local areas and they also have been allocated descriptive names by the House of Commons Library makming it easier to describe areas. These areas have been used to analyse casualties by MSOA region and compare at a country level, similar to as has been done with LAs at the beginning of this document, but hopefully providing more targeted information for a local area.
Analysing the population weighted IMD from LSOA for MSOA for different casualty types, the strongest trends are for Taxi occupant, Mobility scooter rider, Motorcyclist, Pedestrian casualties as shown in the plot below.







The table below shows data for the 43 MSOA areas in Cambridge where casualties live and ranked against other MSOAs nationally. Casualties involved in collisions outside of Cambridge are also included. Between 2010 and 2024, the MSOA with the highest number of casualties was East Barnwell & Abbey with 140 casualties. The most deprived MSOA (by weighted IMD score) was Kings Hedges (IMD 9.9), while the least deprived was Eddington & Castle (IMD 4.6). The highest MSOA regions per capita where casualties live in Cambridge are Longstanton, Swavesey & Oakington, ranked 101 nationally out of 7264 for fatalities per capita, East Barnwell & Abbey ranked 493 for KSIs, East Barnwell & Abbey ranked 377 for Serious casualties.










Between 2010 and 2024, 59.3, NA% of all casualties in Cambridge were male and 40.5, NA% were female. Males also accounted for 60.9, NA% of KSI casualties. The most common age band for casualties was 30-39 (21.1% of all casualties), while the highest KSI rate was among the 30-39 age band (18.1% of all KSI). The table below shows the full breakdown of casualties by age band and sex, with severity and the percentage share of KSI and all casualties.


















Government TAG methodology (described here and here allocates a cost of prevention to each collision. The costs are calculated initially based on casualty severity and then grouped by collision severity. For example, the cost of fatal collisions includes the cost of all casualties (including serious and slight casualties) that occur in fatal collisions. This can be matched in various ways to the collision data. The two methods used for the overall reporting in ONS is to simply match the collision severity recorded in the crash data to a single cost for slight, serious and fatal and also to match based on severity and road type. Using the same methods as in the ONS report, the chart below shows the total cost of prevention for the collisions Cambridge.












The cost of collisions by vehicle involved and by speed limit are shown in the plot below. 30mph roads had the highest total cost with £83,668,057, 208.4% above 20mph. The lowest total cost were NAmph with £24,960







Dividing the total cost on each road by the number of collisions gives an estimate for the average cost of each collision on each road. The lowest (£24,960) were NAmph roads and the highest (£136,963) 60mph roads. NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA% of total collisions took place on NAmph roads, but NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA% of the total cost and 60mph roads had 0, NA% of total collisions but 1, NA% of the total cost. The cost of collisions on 60mph roads are, on average, 5.5 times higher than NAmph roads. The average cost of collisions on 30mph roads was 1, NA times higher than 20mph roads.










Between 2010 and 2024, nationally there were 9327 single-vehicle collisions where a pedestrian was struck on a footway or verge. 81% were people driving Cars, Taxis, Goods vehicles and Buses and 12% were people riding pedal cycles and e-scooters. Of these collisions there were 138 fatalities, 94% of these fatal collisions were with people driving Cars, Taxis, Goods vehicles and Buses and 1% died from collisions with people riding pedal cycles and e-scooters. A further 2300 serious injuries, 79% of the fatal collisions were with people driving Cars, Taxis, Goods vehicles and Buses and 13% from collisions with people riding pedal cycles and e-scooters.
In Cambridge there were 25 reported incidents, resulting in 28 casualties. The vehicle type most commonly involved was Car with 9 collisions and 12 casualties. 48% of the vehicles in collisions were people driving Cars, Taxis, Goods vehicles and Buses and 44% were people riding pedal cycles and escooters. Of these collisions there were 1 fatalities, 100% of these collisions were with people driving Cars, Taxis, Goods vehicles and Buses and 0% from collisions with people riding pedal cycles and e-scooters. A further 3 serious injuries, 33% of which were with people driving Cars, Taxis, Goods vehicles and Buses and 67% from collisions with people riding pedal cycles and e-scooters. The plot below is meant to visually represent a pavement with squares for all the KSI casualties in Cambridge coloured by the vehicle that hit them.
